stories.title: Flipping the Script on “Safe” Haven Bonds
stories.excerpt: Executive Summary The war in Iran has scrambled traditional views of safety in fixed income, pushing investors out of long‑duration Treasuries and broad investment‑grade benchmarks and into floating‑rate, senior‑secured credit. From February 28 through early July, the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN) returned just over 3% on a total‑return basis, compared with losses of about 5% to 6% for long‑Treasury proxies such as the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) and mildly negative or flat performance for core bond indexes. War Reshapes “Safe” Bonds A sector‑level review using liquid ETFs shows the divergence clearly. Since the attacks began on February 28, BKLN’s price and income have produced a total return slightly above 3%, more than double the roughly 1.5% gains in dedicated floating‑rate note strategies such as FLRN and short‑maturity high‑yield funds like SJNK over the same window. Long‑duration Treasuries, by contrast, have fallen around 5% in price terms, as represented by TLT’s performance. Core, investment‑grade exposure, proxied by Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND), has also been modestly negative as rising yields offset coupon income. These moves map closely to changing macro expectations. Market‑implied inflation breakevens and policy‑rate futures have shifted toward a higher‑for‑longer profile since late February, while concerns about rising U.S. federal debt levels have intensified, collectively undermining the traditional safe‑haven role of long Treasuries and boosting demand for floating‑rate credit. Why Floating-Rate Credit Is Winning BKLN holds floating‑rate, senior‑secured loans rated below investment grade and currently offers a trailing 12‑month distribution yield in the mid‑single digits, with a portfolio duration close to zero because coupons reset with short‑term benchmarks. In the current environment, investors appear willing to accept additional credit risk in exchange for coupons that adjust upward if the Fed hikes rates, preserving rea
bullets: Senior floating-rate loans (BKLN) returned 3% since Feb 28; long Treasuries (TLT) fell ~5%
Investor flight from long-duration bonds into floating-rate credit amid higher-for-longer rate expectations
Rising federal debt and inflation breakevens eroding safe-haven appeal of traditional fixed income
stories.summary: Since late February geopolitical shocks, capital has rotated sharply from long-duration Treasuries and core bonds into floating-rate, senior-secured credit. Senior loan ETFs gained 3% while 20+ year Treasury proxies lost 5%. The shift reflects market expectations of persistently elevated rates and concerns about U.S. fiscal sustainability, undermining the traditional safety of long-duration fixed income.
stories.impact: Rising rate expectations and tighter spreads on floating-rate debt reduce refinancing optionality and increase borrowing costs for multifamily developers reliant on fixed-rate, long-duration debt. Projects underwritten at lower cap rates face margin compression; developers with floating-rate bridge or construction debt face headwinds. Expect cap-rate expansion in secondary markets as cost of capital remains elevated across the board.

stories.title: CPC Mortgage Company Secures $42M to Rehabilitate Michigan Housing Property
stories.excerpt: CPC Mortgage Company, a subsidiary of The Community Preservation Corporation, has structured a $42.1 million loan for the preservation and rehabilitation of Parkside Estates and Demby Terraces, a 315-unit affordable housing community in Inkster, Michigan. Developed by Good Housing Partnership in partnership with the Inkster Housing Commission, the properties will undergo a full rehabilitation and conversion under the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development’s Rental Assistance Demonstration program, transitioning from public housing to Section 8. CPC Mortgage Company provided the permanent financing through a Freddie Mac Targeted Affordable Housing forward commitment structured as a tax-exempt loan. The financing structure includes Huntington Bank, which served as the lead arranger and agent of the construction loan, with Deutsche Bank and Associated Bank as co-lenders. Cinnaire acted as the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit syndicator. The rehabilitation will include extensive upgrades to both the site and residential units. Join the industry’s top multifamily decision-makers at Connect Apartments on September 22 in L.A. Hear from the investors, developers, lenders, owners, and advisors shaping today’s apartment market through candid conversations, networking, and market insights. Register today at www.connectapts2026.com. The post CPC Mortgage Company Secures $42M to Rehabilitate Michigan Housing Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: $42.1M permanent financing for 315-unit public housing-to-Section 8 conversion in Inkster, Michigan
Freddie Mac Targeted Affordable Housing forward commitment with LIHTC syndication via Cinnaire
HUD Rental Assistance Demonstration program enabling transition from public to subsidized private operations
stories.summary: CPC Mortgage Company structured $42.1M permanent financing for the full rehabilitation of Parkside Estates and Demby Terraces, a 315-unit affordable housing conversion in Inkster, Michigan. The deal transfers public housing into Section 8 operations under HUD's RAD program, with Freddie Mac providing the forward commitment and tax-credit syndication through Cinnaire. Construction lending came from Huntington Bank, Deutsche Bank, and Associated Bank.
stories.impact: RAD conversions unlock capital for aging public housing stock and improve operational flexibility, but are heavily dependent on Freddie Mac and tax-credit appetite. Multifamily developers eyeing affordable preservation deals should note that subsidy structures (Section 8 tiers, RAD mechanics) now compete directly for investor capital; declining LIHTC prices or reduced GSE appetite would constrain this segment's financing availability across industrial Midwest markets like Michigan.

stories.title: Ice Cream Franchise Coming to Bleecker Street with First NYC Location
stories.excerpt: Scoop N Scootery, a rapidly expanding ice cream franchise with 10 locations across seven states, has selected Bleecker Street for its first New York City location. Lee & Associates NYC arranged the 1,325-square-foot retail lease at 162 Bleecker St., where the company signed a 15-year lease for the ground-floor storefront and plans to open in September 2026. Mark Kapnick, principal at Lee & Associates NYC along with associate director Jordan Cohen, represented landlord Acram Group. Lucas Kooyman of AMPS represented Scoop N Scootery. “New York City is a milestone market for any growing retail brand, and there’s no better place to make that debut than Bleecker Street,” said Kapnick. “Scoop N Scootery wanted a location that immediately establishes its presence in one of the country’s most recognized retail corridors. With exceptional foot traffic, an outstanding mix of food-and-beverage operators and very little available space, Bleecker Street checked every box.” The post Ice Cream Franchise Coming to Bleecker Street with First NYC Location appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Ice cream franchise signs 1,325 SF, 15-year lease at 162 Bleecker Street, NYC
Entry signal into premium NYC retail corridor; company operating 10 units across seven states
Lease valued at prime Bleecker Street rents; limited retail vacancy driving strong landlord position
stories.summary: Scoop N Scootery, a 10-unit ice cream chain, signed a 15-year, 1,325-SF ground-floor lease on Bleecker Street in Manhattan's West Village, opening September 2026. Lee & Associates represented landlord Acram Group. The deal reflects the strong retail leasing momentum and scarcity of available space in one of Manhattan's most iconic retail corridors.
stories.impact: Minimal direct bearing on multifamily development. Story signals robust retail demand and landlord pricing power in prime Manhattan neighborhoods, which historically correlates with strong residential rental demand and high construction costs for mixed-use projects in the Village—relevant only if evaluating mixed-use or ground-floor retail components in Manhattan apartment developments.

stories.title: Pair of Frisco entrepreneurs ordered to pay $400M in damages to Georgetown business over alleged fraud scheme
stories.excerpt: Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered to pay $400 million in damages to a Georgetown-based business after being found liable for breach of contract and fraud by a Williamson County jury.  The pair concocted promotional materials for a luxury development called “The District” that they claimed was going to be built around the San Gabriel River. Krishnan and Gounder were sold the land by the company that sued them, Energy Commissioning Incorporated, owned by Marshall Hussain.  Energy Commissioning was subsequently hired by the pair for $21 million to build necessary infrastructure for a 400-acre, luxury waterfront development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco entrepreneurs Krishnan and Gounder ordered to pay $400M damages for fraud on luxury development scheme
Alleged fraudulent 400-acre 'District' waterfront project marketed to investors; infrastructure contract never realized
SEC previously targeted pair in 2023 for alleged Ponzi scheme; no bond issuance or development materialized
stories.summary: A Williamson County jury found Frisco-based entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder liable for $400M in damages to Georgetown-based Energy Commissioning Inc. over a failed luxury waterfront development scheme. The pair marketed a fake 400-acre 'District' project, contracted $21M in infrastructure services that were never executed, and made only symbolic payments. This follows a 2023 SEC restraining order against the same individuals for an alleged Ponzi scheme.
stories.impact: Negligible direct impact. Fraud case underscores necessity of due diligence on development counterparties and landowners in Texas markets; no housing supply or demand signal. Relevant only as cautionary note on deal sourcing and verification of project legitimacy in competitive land markets.

stories.title: Bascom Acquires Buena Park Apartments as Value-Add Deal
stories.excerpt: The Bascom Group acquired Castlewood Park Apartments, a low-density, 183-unit value-add apartment community in Buena Park. The purchase price was $53,125,000, or $290,301 per unit. Brian Eisendrath, Cameron Chalfant, Jesse Zarouk, and Jake Vitta of IPA Capital Markets arranged the acquisition financing, with Brightspire Capital, Inc. providing the acquisition loan. The IPA Investment Sales team led by Kevin Green and Joe Grabiec represented the undisclosed seller. AMC will provide property management services for the community, while SD-Cap will oversee the planned property renovations of the 1963-vintage community. Lee Nguyen, SVP of operations for Bascom, said, “Castlewood already offers many of the features today’s renter values most, like large floorplans, garage parking, abundant green space, and private yards. By thoughtfully modernizing the community while preserving these unique characteristics, we believe Castlewood will continue to be a highly desirable housing option for North Orange County residents.” The post Bascom Acquires Buena Park Apartments as Value-Add Deal appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Bascom Group acquires 183-unit Castlewood Park (1963 vintage) in Buena Park for $53.1M, $290/unit
Value-add play targeting renovations; Brightspire Capital provided acquisition financing via IPA
North Orange County submarket; large floorplans, parking, and green space already competitive attributes
stories.summary: Bascom Group purchased Castlewood Park Apartments, a 183-unit value-add community in Buena Park, California, for $53.1M ($290K/unit). Brightspire Capital provided the acquisition loan through IPA Capital Markets. The 1963-vintage property will undergo unit and site renovations while retaining its existing strengths: large floor plans, garage parking, green space, and private yards. AMC will manage; SD-Cap will oversee renovations.
stories.impact: Orange County multifamily demonstrates sustained investor appetite for value-add deals at modest per-unit basis; $290K/unit pricing reflects stabilized cash flow in an inflationary environment. Supply of renovated, garden-style units with parking in low-density North OC submarkets remains competitive—reinforces bid pressure on similar 1960s vintage stock and signals limited development upside in walkable, higher-density products unless significant regulatory or zoning shifts occur.

stories.title: Bellhops and bass fishing: Wildhaven developer bets big on nature-centric luxury in Denton
stories.excerpt: A planned luxury resort might be coming to Denton, complete with six miles of horse trails to help bring residents and visitors closer to nature.  Sarasota, Florida-based An Ki Land Development’s managing director, Logan Breen, told the Dallas Business Journal that the new resort they plan on building is intended to combine upscale homes and hotels with “authentic outdoor experiences,” with a total value between $350 and $450 million when completed. The resort, called Wildhaven, is set to span 740 acres on the north side of Denton. 152 homes are set to be built in the resort, ranging from $1.5 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: An Ki Land Development (Sarasota-based) breaking ground on 740-acre Wildhaven mixed-use luxury resort in Denton
$350–450M total project value; 152 homes ($1.5M–$3M+), 100-key hotel, equestrian, spa, fishing amenities
Denton experiencing rapid population and development growth; 400 of 740 acres developed, 300+ preserved for nature
stories.summary: An Ki Land Development, led by Logan Breen, is launching Wildhaven, a 740-acre luxury resort master plan in Denton, Texas, with $350–450M estimated value. The project includes 152 homes (priced $1.5M–$3M+), a 100-key hotel, restaurants, equestrian facilities, spa, and a 10-acre bass fishing pond. Noble Classic Homes will build initial 20 lots; 400 acres developed, 300+ preserved. Wildhaven brand expanding to Colorado and Florida; Denton chosen for first build due to population growth and development activity.
stories.impact: Denton population and employment growth (driven by DFW sprawl) attracts high-end mixed-use resort development, signaling strong demand for lifestyle-oriented residential communities in North Texas suburban markets. For multifamily developers, signals that Denton submarket supports premium rents and affluent in-migration; however, Wildhaven's luxury resort positioning (homes >$1.5M, equestrian, spa) targets income tiers above traditional multifamily—indicates land costs rising and retail/resort competition for prime sites, raising development basis for apartment projects in the corridor.

stories.title: Walker & Dunlop Arranges Refi for BellWorks Fort Monmouth Campus
stories.excerpt: Walker & Dunlop arranged a $60-million bridge loan to refinance Bell Works Fort Monmouth,a mixed-use campus in Tinton Falls, NJ. The Walker & Dunlop Capital Markets Institutional Advisory team led by Dustin Stolly, Aaron Appel, Sean Reimer, Keith Kurland, Jonathan Schwartz, Adam Schwartz, Ari Hirt and Stanley Cayre arranged the bridge loan provided by Hudson Bay Capital on behalf of a joint venture between Inspired by Somerset and H.I.G. Realty Partners, the real estate platform of H.I.G. Capital. Previously home to the Commvault headquarters, the campus has been redeveloped into a mixed-use destination featuring flexible office spaces, retail, dining, coworking spaces and community-focused amenities. It was developed using Inspired by Somerset Development’s “Metroburb” model for creating a self-contained metropolis in suburbia. The original BellWorks development is in nearby Holmdel, NJ. “Bell Works Fort Monmouth is a highly differentiated mixed-use campus that continues to demonstrate strong leasing momentum and long-term value creation,” said Stolly. “The financing recognizes the strength of the asset’s cash flow today while providing flexibility to support its next phase of growth. We’re grateful to Inspired by Somerset, HIG Capital, and Hudson Bay Capital for their partnership on this transaction.” The post Walker & Dunlop Arranges Refi for BellWorks Fort Monmouth Campus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Walker & Dunlop arranges $60M bridge refi for mixed-use BellWorks Fort Monmouth in Tinton Falls, NJ
Hudson Bay Capital lender; joint venture between Inspired by Somerset and H.I.G. Realty Partners
Campus features flexible office, retail, dining, coworking under 'Metroburb' model; strong leasing momentum cited
stories.summary: Walker & Dunlop arranged a $60M bridge loan for BellWorks Fort Monmouth, a mixed-use redeveloped campus in Tinton Falls, New Jersey. Hudson Bay Capital provided the bridge financing to a joint venture between Inspired by Somerset Development and H.I.G. Capital's real estate platform. The asset has been repositioned from the former Commvault HQ into flexible office, retail, dining, and coworking under the 'Metroburb' model; lenders cite strong leasing momentum and next-phase growth potential.
stories.impact: Refinancing of mixed-use suburban campuses signals institutional appetite for 'office conversion' and experiential mixed-use models in secondary markets; bridge financing at likely sub-8% rates suggests moderate leverage and investor confidence in suburban New Jersey leasing fundamentals. For multifamily developers: suburban mixed-use (office + retail + residential) increasingly seen as defensive strategy in Class-B markets; Metroburb positioning indicates operator belief that amenity density and community focus can stabilize mixed-use assets amid office headwinds—relevant for ground-floor and integrated residential components of suburban transit or employment-center developments.

stories.title: Shops at Sunset Place owner seeks taxing district to raise $149M
stories.excerpt: The long-awaited redevelopment of the Shops at Sunset Place in South Miami is seeking a nine-figure boost for the mixed-use project’s infrastructure.  Midtown Development, led by founder Alex Vadia, is requesting Miami-Dade County’s approval to establish a special taxing district that would finance $149 million in infrastructure for its planned 10.2-acre Downtown SoMi mixed-use redevelopment at 5701–5795 Southwest 72nd Street.  The developer proposed creating the Downtown SoMi Community Development District, a special-purpose governmental entity that can finance infrastructure and oversee its maintenance within the development. County commissioners are scheduled to consider the proposal during their July 21 meeting.  The district […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Midtown Development (Alex Vadia) seeks $149M Community Development District for Downtown SoMi mixed-use (10.2 acres)
1,513 units, 200K+ SF retail, 287 hotel rooms, 65.9K SF office, 1,300-seat theater, 2,786 parking spaces
Miami-Dade commissioners to vote July 21; infrastructure construction 2027–Q1 2029; tax-exempt bond financing
stories.summary: Midtown Development is requesting Miami-Dade County approval for a special Community Development District to finance $149M in infrastructure for Downtown SoMi, a 10.2-acre mixed-use redevelopment of Shops at Sunset Place. The 1,513-unit, 200K+ SF retail project includes a 287-room hotel, office, 1,300-seat cinema, and 2,786 parking spaces. If approved, CDD tax-exempt bonds would finance roads, utilities, stormwater, parking garage, and chilled-water systems, with infrastructure construction starting 2027 and completing Q1 2029.
stories.impact: South Miami CDD financing signals developer-led infrastructure bundling to accelerate mixed-use deliveries in suburban infill markets; tax-exempt CDD bonds reduce public subsidy burden while ringfencing construction risk. For multifamily: 1,513 units over 10 acres (~150 units/acre) in South Miami indicates mid-to-high-density suburban positioning; strong retail/hotel integration suggests rent premium relative to pure-play apartments. Florida's permitting and infrastructure capacity (CDD approval, stormwater, chilled-water systemwide) are now critical path items; expect similar district financing to spread across South Florida infill projects, raising soft costs and entitlement risk for competing developments in the Sunset Place/SoMi submarket.

stories.title: Pure Paws Veterinary Care to Relocate to 5K SF at Brooklyn’s 1 Putnam Avenue
stories.excerpt: A pack of Brooklyn veterinarians are taking themselves for a short walk. Pure Paws Veterinary Care is relocating its Brooklyn practice to a larger, nearby location in the Clinton Hill neighborhood. The veterinary office signed a 5,000-square-foot lease at 1 Putnam Avenue, near the intersection of Cambridge Place and Fulton Street. The asking rent for the 10-year lease, signed July 1, was $75 per square foot. Traded first reported the deal. The mixed-use building’s landlord is Abe Betesh. Gabriel and Solomon Betesh of commercial brokerage Momentum Realty Group negotiated the deal on the landlord’s behalf. “We are excited to welcome Pure Paws Veterinary to a new and expanded site to service Clinton Hill,” the brokers said in a joint statement. “We believe in the continued growth of the neighborhood and are proud to play a role in strengthening and enhancing its retail landscape by bringing high-quality businesses to the community.” Pure Paws Veterinary Care currently operates three New York City locations, at 506 West 42nd Street in Manhattan’s Hell’s Kitchen, at 350 Hudson Street in Hudson Square and at 944 Fulton Street in Brooklyn’s Clinton Hill, according to its website. It is unclear when the practice will relocate from its current Clinton Hill address, which is less than a block from its future location.. Babson Real Estate Advisors‘ Richard Bradbury represented the practice in the lease negotiations. Bradbury told Commercial Observer in a statement that Babson was pleased to represent the Pure Paws. “It will give them the opportunity to better serve their loyal and growing customer base for years to come,” Babson said. A representative for Pure Paws did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Pure Paws Veterinary Care signs 5,000 SF, 10-year lease at 1 Putnam Avenue, Brooklyn (Clinton Hill) at $75/SF
Tenant relocating within neighborhood; Momentum Realty Group negotiated on behalf of landlord Abe Betesh
Retail activity in Clinton Hill signals neighborhood retail stability and density of service demand
stories.summary: Pure Paws Veterinary Care leased 5,000 SF at 1 Putnam Avenue in Clinton Hill, Brooklyn, signing a 10-year lease at $75/SF. The veterinary practice is relocating from nearby within the neighborhood to a larger space. Gabriel and Solomon Betesh of Momentum Realty Group represented landlord Abe Betesh. The deal reflects ongoing neighborhood anchoring of essential services and strong neighborhood retail activity.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. Veterinary lease at $75/SF and 10-year lock-in for established tenant signals stable, neighborhood-serving retail demand in Brooklyn's Clinton Hill—relevant only if evaluating retail components or ground-floor anchors in residential developments targeting the same submarket. Retail price ($75/SF) and tenant credit quality (veterinary practice with local customer base) are stable but not indicative of appreciating commercial values in the neighborhood.

stories.title: Bridges Development Group Buys Kingswood Center in Brooklyn for $31M
stories.excerpt: Kingswood Center in Brooklyn’s Midwood neighborhood has a new owner, Commercial Observer has learned. The 229,926-square-foot shopping center at 1630 East 15th Street sold for $31 million to Michael Berfield’s Bridges Development Group, according to broker JLL. The sale marks a steep discount from the $89 million that the shopping center fetched in early 2020, the last time it traded. The three-story, Class B property is currently anchored by T.J. Maxx. A LoopNet listing advertises nearly 100,000 square feet of retail and commercial space available for lease. Kingswood Center was constructed in 2007 and renovated in 2019, according to the listing, and features an on-site parking garage. JLL’s Ethan Stanton, Michael Mazzara and Brendan Maddigan represented the seller, PNC’s Midland Loan Services, in direct negotiations with the buyer. A JLL debt team of Scott Aiese and Alex Staikos secured $34.65 million in acquisition financing for the buyer. The firm declined to disclose the lender. The financing will allow Bridges Development Group to execute a business plan of investing in the property and leasing its vacant space, according to a JLL spokesperson. Midland took possession of the property from its former owner, retail shopping REIT Urban Edge Properties, in 2023. The REIT fell behind on a $66 million commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) loan used to acquire the center in February 2020. Following the ill-timed purchase, the shopping center’s annual net operating income fell to $1.9 million and occupancy declined to 35 percent, The Real Deal reported at the time. Urban Edge Properties still owns the nearby Kingswood Crossing at 1715 East 13th Street, according to its website. Spokespeople for Bridges Development Group and PNC did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bridges Development Group acquires 229,926 SF Kingswood Center (Midwood, Brooklyn) for $31M, 65% discount from 2020 valuation
T.J. Maxx anchored; 100K SF retail/commercial available; JLL secured $34.65M acquisition financing
Class-B 2007-built, 2019-renovated; on-site parking; distressed retail asset repurposing cycle
stories.summary: Bridges Development Group purchased Kingswood Center, a 229,926-SF shopping center in Brooklyn's Midwood neighborhood, for $31M—a significant 65% discount from its $89M sale in early 2020. The T.J. Maxx-anchored center has ~100K SF of available retail/commercial space. JLL arranged $34.65M acquisition financing for the buyer. The 2007-built, 2019-renovated property includes on-site parking, and Bridges will execute a business plan to stabilize and reposition the asset.
stories.impact: Distressed retail asset trading at fire-sale valuation signals persistent headwinds for traditional suburban shopping centers; Bridges' acquisition at 35% of 2020 value and substantial available space availability indicate either repositioning to experiential/service retail or value-add mixed-use (residential ground floor, conversion). For multifamily developers: suburban Brooklyn shopping centers now tradeable at basement valuations and may offer land or ground-floor redevelopment opportunities; however, weak retail fundamentals (100K+ SF vacant, anchor-dependent model) suggest development feasibility hinges on residential conversion or experience-driven anchors (food, wellness, entertainment)—not traditional retail recovery.

stories.title: Data Intelligence Firm Curinos Takes 14K SF at Marx Realty’s 10 Grand Central
stories.excerpt: Curinos, a global data intelligence firm serving the financial industry, is relocating to Marx Realty’s 10 Grand Central after signing a 14,000-square-foot lease, Commercial Observer has learned. Curinos will move from its current office about two blocks north at 485 Lexington Avenue, though it is unclear when the move will happen. The length of the lease was not disclosed. Asking rents at 10 Grand Central — which is now 98 percent leased, according to the landlord — ranges from $85 to $135 per square foot. Adam Ardise and Rachel Rosenfeld from Cushman & Wakefield represented Curinos in the lease, while JLL’s Mitchell Konsker, Thomas Swartz, Carlee Palmer and Margaux Kelleher represented Marx Realty. Spokespeople for C&W and JLL did not immediately respond to CO’s requests for comment. “The robust leasing activity at 10 Grand Central demonstrates the ongoing appeal of thoughtfully designed workplaces and white glove service that combine to give companies an elevated office experience,” Craig Deitelzweig, CEO of Marx Realty, said in a statement. “Curinos’ decision to relocate within the Grand Central market speaks to the differentiated hospitality-infused experience we’ve created at 10 Grand Central and further strengthens the building’s exceptional tenant roster,” Deitelzweig added. 10 Grand Central is a hospitality-infused office building that offers tenants a variety of amenities, including a reservable house car for transportation, a lounge and cafe on the seventh floor, a 1930s French garden-style outdoor terrace, and a newly opened speakeasy-style restaurant called Highball Ltd. Other tenants at 10 Grand Central include data center developer Edged, bank holding company Merchants Bancorp and research firm Green Street Advisors. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Curinos (data intelligence firm) leases 14,000 SF at Marx Realty's 10 Grand Central, Manhattan, at $85–135/SF range
Relocating from 485 Lexington Avenue (two blocks north); 10 Grand Central now 98% leased
Office market signal: financial-services support tenants driving Midtown office stability and rents
stories.summary: Curinos, a global data intelligence firm serving the financial services industry, signed a 14,000-SF lease at Marx Realty's 10 Grand Central in Manhattan. The firm is relocating from 485 Lexington Avenue, two blocks north. Asking rents at 10 Grand Central range from $85–135/SF, and the building is now 98% leased. Cushman & Wakefield represented Curinos; JLL represented Marx Realty.
stories.impact: Negligible for multifamily. Office leasing velocity and rent ranges at 10 Grand Central signal near-full occupancy in Midtown trophy assets, suggesting commercial real estate confidence in Manhattan's financial district and ability to retain white-collar, high-wage tenants (data/fintech). Not indicative of apartment rental demand, though financial-services job concentration in Midtown does support overall demand for luxury/premium rentals; secondary effect already priced into Manhattan submarket fundamentals.

stories.title: Iris Escarrá of Greenberg Traurig: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: As co-chair of the land use practice at law firm Greenberg Traurig, Iris Escarrá has worked on a number of high-profile projects. In one recent example, Escarrá was part of the team that won approvals for Miami Freedom Park, the mixed-use development near Miami International Airport that includes Nu Stadium, the 26,700-seat home of Major League Soccer’s Inter Miami CF. Before the stadium opened in April, she led the team that guided the development team through environmental remediation, code compliance and traffic and aviation issues. The following conversation has been edited for length and clarity.   Commercial Observer: Now that Nu Stadium is open and Inter Miami is playing matches there, what lessons did you learn from the project? Iris Escarrá: There’s so many. Some of them I have to cover over drinks. Some of them I can cover here. The stadium is actually one component. It’s 12 acres of a 73-acre complex that’s mixed-use. So while the stadium is the biggest to come in, we have retail coming in, hotel, office, and so forth that we’re also working on. It’s very exciting because that’s what’s actually going to create the whole district. Inter Miami has 17 home games, and they’ll have other events there, concerts, soccer games for high schools, but it’s never going to be a 365-day-a-year building. So it’s really the other uses around it that are going to create the district. And those uses around it are actually what helps with some of the traffic items because the restaurants and the retail are open a few hours before the match, so if you want to go and have lunch before the game, you could do that. If you want to go after the game, go hang out. So what happens is that the mixed-use district around the stadiums is what’s really creating the whole district. When are those other uses going to be completed? We have a few buildings that are in for permitting now, some food and beverage, some entertainment. T
bullets: Iris Escarrá (Greenberg Traurig land use co-chair) led entitlements for Miami Freedom Park mixed-use complex
Nu Stadium (26,700-seat MLS venue) opened April 2024; 73-acre mixed-use site includes retail, hotel, office
Complex entitlement narrative: environmental remediation, code compliance, aviation/traffic issues overcome for stadium anchor
stories.summary: Iris Escarrá, co-chair of Greenberg Traurig's land use practice, led the entitlements team for Miami Freedom Park, a 73-acre mixed-use development anchored by Nu Stadium, the 26,700-seat home of Inter Miami CF (MLS). Nu Stadium opened in April 2024. The development includes 12 acres for the stadium plus retail, hotel, and office components on the remaining site. Entitlements navigated environmental remediation, code compliance, and aviation/traffic coordination near Miami International Airport.
stories.impact: Miami mixed-use sports-anchored development signals institutional confidence in South Florida infill redevelopment and MLS-driven placemaking value. For multifamily: stadium-anchored and mixed-use resorts create premium rental demand (hotel workers, sports fans, office tenants); however, complex entitlements (airport proximity, remediation) and long lead times (multi-year approvals) mean developers targeting similar South Florida stadium/event-adjacent sites should expect elevated soft costs, longer predevelopment windows, and higher basis—relevant for evaluating downtown Miami/Brickell competitive positioning against suburban DFW or secondary markets with faster entitlement paths.

stories.title: Hochul taps developers to build $1B Hell’s Kitchen towers
stories.excerpt: The state has tapped three New York developers to build two towers with more than 1,100 homes on a publicly owned parking lot in Hell’s Kitchen across from the USS Intrepid museum. Gov. Kathy Hochul selected The Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers and Mural Real Estate to construct the highrises with 1,127 condos and apartments at 621 West 45th Street, Crain’s New York reported.  Plans for the $1 billion project, dubbed Hudson Landing, include two connected towers containing 1.3 million square feet on a 1.2-acre lot across from Pier 86 on the Hudson River. Of the 1,127 units, 108 would be […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Gov. Hochul selects Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers, Mural Real Estate for $1B Hell's Kitchen project at 621 West 45th
1,127 units (108 condos, 28 affordable-for-sale, 338 affordable rentals at 40–130% AMI) on former state-owned parking lot
State override of local zoning via Redeveloping Underused Sites policy; two towers, 1.3M SF, pedestrian bridge to Intrepid Museum
stories.summary: Gov. Kathy Hochul has selected Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers, and Mural Real Estate Group to develop Hudson Landing, a $1B mixed-use project on a state-owned parking lot at 621 West 45th Street in Hell's Kitchen. The development will deliver 1,127 units (108 for-sale condos with 28 affordable, 338 affordable rental apartments at 40–130% AMI) across two towers spanning 1.3M SF on 1.2 acres. The state is overriding local zoning rules under its 2024 Redeveloping Underused Sites housing policy. A pedestrian bridge will connect to the Intrepid Museum; retail, restaurants, and ~10K SF public park also planned.
stories.impact: New York state override of Hell's Kitchen local zoning to mandate mixed-income housing on public land signals aggressive state-led supply expansion using executive power to circumvent local resistance. For multifamily developers: implies (1) future public-land conveyances may bypass traditional RFP rigor; (2) income-restricted affordable percentages will likely be templated at 30%+ in NYC projects going forward, raising underwriting complexity; (3) West Side Manhattan waterfront rentals face new competition from state-catalyzed supply. Developers should monitor replication of this zoning-override model in other NY metros and expect pressure to increase affordability set-asides in Manhattan developments, compressing unregulated unit economics.

stories.title: Hochul Selects Developers to Build 1,127 Housing Units in Hell’s Kitchen
stories.excerpt: A parking lot near the Intrepid Museum along the Hell’s Kitchen waterfront is about to become housing. Developers Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers and Mural Real Estate Group have been chosen by Gov. Kathy Hochul to transform the 50,584-square-foot parking lot at 621 West 45th Street into two residential towers containing about 1,127 new homes, the governor’s office announced Wednesday. Crain’s New York Business first reported the news. Hochul announced in February of last year that she wanted to turn the state-owned parking lot along the West Side Highway — which is currently being used by the Intrepid Museum — into a residential development to help dent the city’s housing shortage. “The West Side of Manhattan has a storied history as a vibrant, inclusive community, and I’m committed to strengthening that legacy by building for a more affordable future,” Hochul said in the 2025 announcement. “By transforming this underutilized state-owned property into a dynamic mixed-use development with a significant affordable housing component, we’re taking aim at low housing supply in a high-demand area and building a stronger community for residents in Hell’s Kitchen and beyond.” Gotham, Fisher and Mural will redevelop the lot into two connected residential towers that will feature 1,127 units, with some 338 being set aside as affordable housing for those earning between 40 percent and 130 percent of the area median income, Crain’s reported. The total will include 108 for-sale condo units, with some 28 set aside as affordable housing. “Gotham has spent decades partnering with government, community organizations, and mission-driven institutions on the West Side of Manhattan to deliver housing that serves New Yorkers across the income spectrum, and this project builds on that legacy,” David Picket, CEO of Gotham, said in a statement to Commercial Observer. “This transformative development will deliver hundreds of permanently affordable homes, including homes for
bullets: Same project as Article 13: Hochul selects Gotham, Fisher Brothers, Mural for Hell's Kitchen 1,127-unit towers
621 West 45th Street, $1B Hudson Landing; two towers on former state-owned parking lot; zoning override via state policy
~338 affordable rental units at 40–130% AMI; public park, retail, restaurants; mixed-use waterfront development
stories.summary: Developers Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers, and Mural Real Estate Group have been selected by Gov. Kathy Hochul to develop two residential towers containing approximately 1,127 homes on a 50,584-SF state-owned parking lot at 621 West 45th Street in Hell's Kitchen. The $1B Hudson Landing project features mixed-income housing, with 338 rental units reserved for households earning 40–130% of area median income. The development includes retail, restaurants, and a public park. State zoning override allows the project to proceed despite local manufacturing zoning.
stories.impact: Duplicate of Article 13; see prior analysis. State-led zoning override and public-land conveyance model will likely proliferate in NY metro; multifamily developers should expect regulatory pressure to increase affordability set-asides and anticipate state-catalyzed competition in Manhattan submarkets, particularly underutilized public/industrial sites zoned for manufacturing.

stories.title: PTM Partners, Peacock launch subsidy-free workforce housing platform
stories.excerpt: Florida-based PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a platform designed to deliver lower-cost housing without relying on government aid.  The venture targets middle-income renters earning between 80 and 120 percent of the area median income, cutting development costs by handling much of the design, construction and material procurement in-house, according to a news release.  By using a standardized building design and cold-formed steel purchased directly from suppliers, Inception Housing can cut hard construction costs by about 25 percent compared to similar products, PTM Partners CEO Michael Tillman said. The platform’s strategy delivers buildings in six to seven months, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: PTM Partners (Florida) and Peacock Capital launch Inception Housing platform targeting 80–120% AMI workforce rentals
Standardized design, cold-formed steel procurement reduce hard costs 25%; construction time 6–7 months (50% faster)
One project underway: Magnolia Flats (South Orlando, 74 units + 14 existing townhomes); leveraging Florida Live Local Act
stories.summary: PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a platform delivering subsidies-free workforce housing (80–120% AMI) through standardized design and direct material procurement. By using cold-formed steel and in-house construction management, Inception cuts hard costs ~25% and reduces construction timelines to 6–7 months (50% faster than traditional stick-build). South Florida faces a 30,460-unit shortage; statewide Florida shortage is ~120,850 units. Inception's first project, Magnolia Flats in South Orlando, will deliver 74 new units plus 14 existing townhome acquisitions.
stories.impact: Modular/standardized construction platform targeting workforce housing (80–120% AMI, unsubsidized) signals market recognition of affordability gap and attempt to compete on construction speed and cost. For multifamily developers: Inception's 25% cost reduction and 50% faster delivery suggest competitive pressure on basis and timeline for mid-market developments in Florida; however, model relies on high-volume standardization, land availability, and Live Local zoning incentives—less applicable in supply-constrained or high-cost metros (NYC, LA). Relevant for regional developers with pipeline in Florida and Southeast; developers in expensive urban markets should expect margin pressure if modular/factory-built technology penetrates, but geographic and zoning constraints may limit displacement in constrained infill markets.

stories.title: Dallas apartment pipeline gets a boost with pair of financing deals
stories.excerpt: Two developers of a pair of apartment complexes in the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex recently secured financing for their separate projects.  Endeavor Real Estate locked in financing for their luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney Avenue named Lucille. Additionally, StreetLights Residential snagged $54 million in equity financing for an apartment tower in Plano, Texas, which will be built in the redevelopment of The Park at Legacy at 6501 Legacy Drive.  Lucille will feature 265 units across 22 stories in Uptown, according to the Dallas Morning News. The development is helmed by Austin-based Endeavor and equity partner Canyon Partners Real Estate. Construction on […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor Real Estate secures financing for Lucille luxury high-rise: 265 units, 22 stories, Uptown Dallas (2700 McKinney Ave.)
StreetLights Residential lands $54M equity for 100+ unit tower in Plano (Park at Legacy redevelopment, 6501 Legacy Drive)
Dallas-Fort Worth pipeline accelerating; Lucille construction begins summer 2025, delivery late 2028; Northmarq structures StreetLights financing
stories.summary: Two major Dallas-Fort Worth apartment financing deals closed: (1) Endeavor Real Estate secured financing for Lucille, a 265-unit, 22-story luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney Avenue in Uptown Dallas, with equity partner Canyon Partners Real Estate. The project spans 320K SF (10K retail, rooftop pool, observation deck, 4-level parking); construction starts summer 2025, delivery late 2028. (2) StreetLights Residential secured $54M equity financing for an apartment tower at The Park at Legacy redevelopment (6501 Legacy Drive, Plano). Northmarq structured StreetLights' five-year financing. Both projects target affluent professionals and office workers in growth corridors.
stories.impact: Dallas-Fort Worth multifamily financing acceleration reflects sustained institutional equity appetite and strong leasing fundamentals in Uptown and suburban employment centers (Plano Legacy Park office-to-mixed-use conversion). For developers: (1) Uptown Dallas luxury rents support 265+ unit high-rises with premium amenities (rooftop pool, observation deck), signaling continued pricing power in CBD/financial district; (2) Park at Legacy redevelopment (107-acre office campus conversion) indicates suburban Dallas office-to-residential conversion play gaining traction—expect more suburban office park repurposing in DFW and similar tech/service-employment metros. Financing availability and strong equity participation signal moderate cap-rate compression in both submarkets; developers should expect competitive bidding on Uptown land/entitled sites and early-mover advantage in office campus conversion projects before valuations fully reflect conversion demand.
